**The Humility the Climate Debate Has Forgotten**
*by Dr. Alan Carter*

For forty years I have studied paleoclimate. I have pulled ice cores from Antarctic shelves, sifted through Holocene sediment records, and traced the isotopic fingerprints of ancient atmospheres. And I have watched, with growing dismay, as a field I love has been conscripted into a political campaign that insists the question is closed. It is not closed. It has barely been opened.

The central claim of anthropogenic-warming advocacy is that human carbon dioxide emissions are the principal driver of the warming observed since the mid-twentieth century. This claim rests on a particular reading of the ice core record—specifically, the tight correlation between CO₂ and temperature over the past 800,000 years as preserved in the Vostok and EPICA cores. Correlation, we are told, is causation. CO₂ rises, temperature rises.

But look closer. Look at the chronology. In core after core, across glacial termination after glacial termination, the temperature rises *first*, and CO₂ follows—by centuries, sometimes by the better part of a millennium. The Vostok record shows lags of roughly 800 years at the close of the last ice age. Whatever drove those ancient warmings, it was not carbon dioxide, because the carbon dioxide was not yet there. The gas arrived late to its own party.

What does drive the Earth's temperature, then? The honest answer is: many things, most of them natural, and most of them poorly quantified. The Milankovitch cycles—the slow wobble and tilt of our planet's orbit—redistribute solar energy across latitudes on tens-of-thousands-of-years timescales and set the rhythm of the glacials. Solar output itself varies; the Maunder Minimum coincided with the coldest depths of the Little Ice Age, and the mid-twentieth-century grand solar maximum coincided with a warming world. Ocean oscillations—the Pacific Decadal Oscillation, the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation—redistribute heat between surface and deep on cycles of sixty years or more, long enough to be mistaken for trends by a civilization with only 150 years of thermometer records.

Against this symphony of natural forcings, we are asked to believe that a trace gas, which constitutes four parts in ten thousand of the atmosphere, has suddenly become the baton conductor of the entire climate system. We are asked to believe this on the basis of models—models that cannot reproduce the Medieval Warm Period, cannot explain the pause in warming from roughly 1998 to 2013, and cannot hindcast the early-twentieth-century warming that occurred before human emissions were significant.

I do not deny that carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas. It is. I do not deny that human activity has added to the atmospheric burden. It has. What I deny—and what any honest reading of the paleoclimate record compels one to deny—is that this modest human contribution has displaced the sun, the oceans, and the orbit as the dominant driver of a planetary system four and a half billion years in the making. The climate has always changed. It changed before us, and it will change after us. The warming of the last century is well within the envelope of natural variability, and the ice cores themselves—read forward rather than backward—tell us that CO₂ is a passenger, not a pilot.

The alarmism of our present moment is not science. It is a story science is being made to tell. When temperatures rose in the 1980s, we were warned of catastrophe by 2000. When that did not come, the deadline moved to 2020, then 2030, then 2050. The goalposts migrate because the underlying claim is not falsifiable in the way real scientific claims must be. A theory that explains every warm year, every cold year, every storm, every calm as confirmation of itself is not a theory. It is a worldview.

I am sometimes asked whether I feel isolated. I do not. I feel, rather, like a man who has kept reading the data when others stopped. The humility that made science great was the willingness to say *we do not yet know*. That humility has been traded for certainty, and certainty, in science, is almost always the first sign that something has gone wrong.

If it's useful for your narrative, the specific scientific rebuttal to Carter's centerpiece argument is that the CO₂ lag at glacial terminations is entirely consistent with—and actually predicted by—mainstream climate science: orbital forcing initiates warming, which releases CO₂ from oceans, which then amplifies the warming as a feedback. CO₂ can be both a feedback in one context and a forcing in another, and the current situation (CO₂ rising *first*, from fossil sources identifiable by their isotopic signature) is the forcing case. A character who opposes Carter in your story would likely press exactly this point.

The pattern of them—always in the direction that steepens the trend—is not the pattern one expects from neutral corrections. Neutral corrections scatter.

Now set this measurement problem beside the paleoclimate record, and the edifice begins to totter.

This claim rests on a particular reading of the ice cores—specifically, the correlation between CO₂ and temperature preserved in the Vostok and EPICA records. Correlation, we are told, is causation. CO₂ rises, temperature rises.

In core after core, across glacial termination after glacial termination, the temperature rises *first*, and CO₂ follows—by centuries, sometimes by the better part of a millennium. Whatever drove those ancient warmings, it was not carbon dioxide, because the carbon dioxide was not yet there. The gas arrived late to its own party.

What does drive the Earth's temperature, then? The Milankovitch cycles set the rhythm of the glacials. Solar output varies; the Maunder Minimum coincided with the coldest depths of the Little Ice Age, and the mid-twentieth-century grand solar maximum coincided with a warming world. Ocean oscillations—the Pacific Decadal Oscillation, the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation—redistribute heat between surface and deep on cycles of sixty years or more, long enough to be mistaken for trends by a civilization with only 150 years of thermometer records, and those thermometers, as I have said, sited increasingly in the heart of our own waste heat.

Against this symphony of natural forcings, and against a surface record compromised at its foundation, we are asked to believe that a trace gas of four parts in ten thousand has become the baton conductor of the entire climate system. We are asked to believe it on the basis of models that cannot reproduce the Medieval Warm Period, cannot explain the pause from roughly 1998 to 2013, and cannot hindcast the early-twentieth-century warming that preceded significant human emissions.

I do not deny that carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas. I do not deny that human activity has added to the atmospheric burden. What I deny—what any honest reading of the paleoclimate record and any honest audit of the surface stations compels one to deny—is that this modest human contribution has displaced the sun, the oceans, and the orbit as the dominant driver of a system four and a half billion years old. The climate has always changed. Much of what we now measure as warming is the warmth of our own cities, recorded by instruments we have allowed our cities to swallow.

The humility that made science great was the willingness to say *we do not yet know*. That humility has been traded for certainty, and certainty, in science, is almost always the first sign that something has gone wrong.

**The Humility the Climate Debate Has Forgotten**
*by Dr. Alan Carter*

For forty years I have studied paleoclimate. I have pulled ice cores from Antarctic shelves, sifted through Holocene sediment records, and traced the isotopic fingerprints of ancient atmospheres. And I have watched, with growing dismay, as a field I love has been conscripted into a political campaign that insists the question is closed. It is not closed. It has barely been opened.

Begin with a question that ought to trouble anyone who takes the warming narrative at face value: *where, exactly, is the warming being measured?*

Walk the siting reports. You will find thermometers beside jet blast fences at regional airports. You will find them on rooftops tarred black against the sun, beside air-conditioner exhausts, in parking lots that did not exist when the station was commissioned in 1910. You will find them in towns that have grown from villages of two thousand into cities of two hundred thousand over the life of the record. This is the Urban Heat Island effect—documented, measurable, and rarely honestly confronted. Compare the genuinely rural stations, surrounded by pasture and forest rather than runways and retail parks, and the dramatic rise flattens. The planetary fever begins to look less like a fever and more like the thermal fingerprint of twentieth-century urbanization mistaken for a global trend.

The adjustments do not reassure me. Our records are not raw; they are "homogenized," corrected, infilled. Stations that once cooled have, after adjustment, warmed. Past temperatures have been revised downward; recent ones upward. Each correction may be defensible in isolation. The pattern of them—always in the direction that steepens the trend—is not what neutral corrections produce. Neutral corrections scatter.

Now consider the models—the instruments on which so much of the alarm depends.

For three decades we have been told the models are reliable enough to guide civilizational policy. For three decades the models have run hot. The ensemble mean of the CMIP projections has consistently predicted more warming than the satellites and the radiosondes have recorded. John Christy's tropospheric comparisons—model trend against observed trend, over the tropical mid-troposphere where greenhouse theory predicts its clearest signature—show a discrepancy that has widened, not narrowed, as the observational record has grown. When roughly the first decade and a half of this century saw surface warming slow to a crawl even as atmospheric CO₂ climbed steadily past 400 parts per million, not one of the major models had forecast it. Papers were written afterward to explain it—deep-ocean heat uptake, aerosol forcing, volcanic aerosols, natural variability suddenly rediscovered—but explanation after the fact is not prediction. A theory that can accommodate any outcome has forfeited the power to predict any.

The implication is one the field has been reluctant to speak plainly. If the models run hot, the parameter making them run hot is almost certainly equilibrium climate sensitivity—the warming expected from a doubling of CO₂. The IPCC's long-standing range of roughly 1.5 to 4.5 degrees per doubling has been, in practice, defended at its upper end. The observational energy-balance studies—Lewis and Curry most prominently—point to a value near the lower bound, perhaps below it. A climate sensitivity of 1.5 degrees, or less, describes a different world entirely than a climate sensitivity of 4. It describes a world in which the warming from plausible emissions pathways is modest, manageable, and likely smaller than the natural variability that has moved the climate through the Medieval Warm Period, the Little Ice Age, and the dozen Holocene oscillations before them.

Set this beside the paleoclimate record, and the edifice totters. The Vostok and EPICA ice cores show, across every glacial termination, that temperature rises *first*, and CO₂ follows by centuries. Whatever drove those ancient warmings, it was not carbon dioxide, because the carbon dioxide was not yet there. The gas arrived late to its own party.

What did drive them? The Milankovitch cycles set the rhythm of the glacials. Solar output varies; the Maunder Minimum coincided with the coldest depths of the Little Ice Age, and the mid-twentieth-century grand solar maximum coincided with a warming world. Ocean oscillations—the Pacific Decadal Oscillation, the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation—redistribute heat on cycles long enough to be mistaken for trends by a civilization with only 150 years of thermometers, and thermometers, as I have said, sited increasingly in the heart of our own waste heat.

I grant that carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas; I will not pretend otherwise, and no serious skeptic does. What I deny is the leap from that laboratory fact to the claim that this molecule—present at four parts in ten thousand, its logarithmic effect already largely saturated in the bands that matter—has displaced the sun, the oceans, and the orbit as the dominant driver of a system four and a half billion years old. The laboratory effect is real. Its magnitude in the actual, coupled, feedback-laden climate system is what is at issue, and every honest check against observation—the pause, the tropospheric discrepancy, the energy-balance estimates, the ice cores read forward—points toward a sensitivity small enough that natural variability remains the larger hand on the wheel.

The humility that made science great was the willingness to say *we do not yet know*. That humility has been traded for certainty, and certainty, in science, is almost always the first sign that something has gone wrong.

---

# Natural Cycles, Not Human Activity: Reconsidering the Dominant Narrative on Climate Change

## Introduction

Earth's climate has never been static. Long before smokestacks and SUVs, the planet cycled through dramatic warming and cooling episodes driven entirely by natural forces — orbital mechanics, solar variability, ocean oscillations, and volcanic activity. The modern insistence that human CO₂ emissions are the principal driver of recent warming rests on a causal assumption that the paleoclimate record directly contradicts: ice core data from Antarctica show that across hundreds of thousands of years, CO₂ concentrations have risen *after* temperatures rose, not before. This single fact reverses the causal arrow at the heart of the anthropogenic narrative. Combined with the evidence from solar cycles, ocean oscillations, the serious biases contaminating the surface temperature record, and observational studies showing that the climate's sensitivity to CO₂ is far lower than alarmist models assume, the conclusion is clear: natural factors are the primary drivers of recent climate change, and the alarmism built atop the anthropogenic narrative is not supported by the historical or observational evidence.

## Natural Climate Variability is Enormous

The geological record documents climate swings that dwarf anything observed in the industrial era. The Medieval Warm Period (roughly 950–1250 AD) allowed Norse settlers to farm in Greenland and vineyards to flourish in England. It was followed by the Little Ice Age (roughly 1300–1850), during which the Thames froze regularly, glaciers advanced across Europe, and famine gripped entire regions. The Holocene Climatic Optimum, some 6,000 to 9,000 years ago, was warmer than the present across much of the globe. Previous interglacials were warmer still, with sea levels substantially higher than today — all without industrial CO₂.

These episodes prove that the climate system produces large fluctuations entirely on its own. Any honest attribution of modern warming must first rule out these natural drivers, not assume them away.

## The Vostok Ice Cores: CO₂ Follows, It Does Not Lead

The Vostok ice cores drilled in East Antarctica provide one of the longest and most detailed records of atmospheric composition and temperature available to science, reaching back more than 400,000 years and capturing four full glacial-interglacial cycles. The record shows an unmistakable pattern: temperature rises first, and atmospheric CO₂ rises afterward, with a lag typically estimated at several hundred years — often cited as around 600 to 800 years at the end of glacial periods.

This lag is devastating to the claim that CO₂ is the master control knob of Earth's climate. If CO₂ were the primary driver of temperature, we would expect CO₂ increases to precede warming. Instead, the ice cores show the opposite: something else warms the planet first, and the oceans — which hold enormous dissolved CO₂ reserves whose solubility decreases as they warm — then release CO₂ into the atmosphere as a consequence. CO₂, in other words, acts as a feedback, not as the initiator. The same chemistry operates in reverse during cooling periods, with CO₂ declining only after temperatures fall.

The textbook narrative treats rising CO₂ as the cause and rising temperature as the effect, but the longest and best-resolved natural record we possess shows precisely the reverse relationship.

## Solar and Orbital Forcings

Milankovitch cycles — slow variations in Earth's orbit and axial tilt — drive the glacial-interglacial rhythm captured in the Vostok record. These orbital changes alter the distribution of solar energy reaching Earth and are the leading candidate for what actually initiates the warming that precedes CO₂ rise in the ice cores. On shorter timescales, solar output varies measurably. The Maunder Minimum (1645–1715), a period of near-absent sunspots, coincided with the deepest cold of the Little Ice Age. The Dalton Minimum in the early 1800s produced similar results. By contrast, the 20th century coincided with the Modern Maximum — among the most active solar periods in centuries — precisely when the warming now attributed to CO₂ accelerated. The correlation between solar activity and terrestrial climate across centuries is empirical and robust.

## Ocean Oscillations

The oceans hold vastly more heat than the atmosphere, and their internal oscillations redistribute that heat on timescales of decades. The Pacific Decadal Oscillation, Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation, and El Niño–Southern Oscillation all produce substantial swings in surface temperatures. The strong late-20th-century warming coincided with a positive PDO phase and major El Niño events, notably 1997–98. The subsequent slowdown in surface warming during the 2000s — which caught climate modelers off guard and required extensive post-hoc revision — demonstrates that natural ocean variability is not a minor footnote but a dominant force that existing models handle poorly.

## The Urban Heat Island Effect Skews the Record

Claims of unprecedented warming rest heavily on the surface temperature record, yet that record is profoundly contaminated by the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect. Cities are warmer than surrounding countryside — often by several degrees — because asphalt, concrete, and buildings absorb and retain heat, waste heat from vehicles and air conditioning is vented into the air, and vegetation that once provided evaporative cooling has been replaced by impervious surfaces.

This matters enormously because many long-running weather stations began in rural or semi-rural settings and have since been engulfed by urban and suburban development. Surveys of U.S. stations have documented monitoring sites placed next to air conditioning exhausts, parking lots, and buildings — conditions guaranteed to produce warming readings unrelated to global climate. While adjustments are applied to account for UHI, those adjustments are themselves contested, opaque, and in some cases appear to increase rather than decrease recent trends. Satellite-based temperature measurements, which are not subject to the same siting problems, have generally shown less warming than surface records, particularly in the tropical troposphere where greenhouse theory predicts the strongest signal.

When a meaningful portion of the "global warming" signal may reflect the growth of cities around thermometers rather than a genuine change in the atmosphere, the confidence placed in attribution studies is badly misplaced.

## The Case Against Alarmism: Low Climate Sensitivity

The catastrophic framing of climate change depends almost entirely on a single contested number: Equilibrium Climate Sensitivity (ECS), the estimated long-run warming from a doubling of atmospheric CO₂. The IPCC has historically presented ECS ranges centered around 3°C per doubling, with the upper tail stretching toward 4.5°C or higher — values that generate the alarming projections driving policy debates. These higher estimates come predominantly from general circulation models, not from direct observation.

Observational, energy-budget-based studies tell a very different story. Research by Nicholas Lewis and Judith Curry, published in peer-reviewed journals, has estimated ECS in the range of roughly 1.5 to 1.8°C per doubling — less than half of what the high-end model estimates suggest. Similar observational analyses by Otto and colleagues, as well as work by Lindzen and Choi on tropical feedback processes, have likewise pointed toward a lower sensitivity than models assume. The core reason is that models appear to exaggerate positive feedbacks, particularly from clouds and water vapor, while real-world data on the actual ratio of warming to forcing over the industrial era simply does not support high sensitivity values.

The consequences are decisive. If ECS is near 1.5°C rather than 3°C or higher, then a doubling of CO₂ — a threshold the world is not close to reaching — produces modest, manageable, and largely benign warming. The entire edifice of climate alarmism, from urgent net-zero mandates to predictions of civilizational collapse, depends on a high-sensitivity assumption that observational data does not support.

Alongside this, the predictive track record is poor. Climate models have consistently run hotter than measured temperatures. Predictions of ice-free Arctic summers, snow-free European winters, and imminent coastal inundation have repeatedly failed to materialize on schedule. Meanwhile, deaths from climate-related disasters have fallen dramatically over the past century, global crop yields continue to rise, and satellite imagery shows significant greening of the planet, aided by CO₂ fertilization. These are not the signatures of an unfolding catastrophe.

## Conclusion

Five independent lines of evidence converge on a single verdict. The Vostok ice cores show that CO₂ follows temperature by hundreds of years, inverting the causal claim on which the anthropogenic theory rests. Solar cycles — from the Maunder and Dalton Minima to the 20th-century Modern Maximum — track the actual pattern of cooling and warming far more faithfully than CO₂ ever has. Ocean oscillations in the Pacific and Atlantic move heat on decadal scales that swamp greenhouse forcing and that climate models conspicuously fail to capture. The Urban Heat Island effect contaminates the surface temperature record that alarmist claims depend upon, with satellite data consistently showing less warming than ground stations placed beside parking lots and air conditioners. And observational estimates of Equilibrium Climate Sensitivity place the warming from doubled CO₂ at roughly half the value required to sustain catastrophic projections, pulling the foundation out from beneath the entire alarmist edifice.

Taken together, these are not five separate doubts but a single coherent refutation. The anthropogenic theory of recent climate change is fundamentally flawed: its causality is reversed, its temperature record is corrupted, its dominant drivers are mislabeled as noise, and its central parameter is empirically overstated. Natural factors are the primary drivers of the climate changes observed over the past century. The so-called settled science is not settled, not because the question is merely uncertain, but because the evidence points decisively the other way — and a modestly warmer, CO₂-enriched world is, on balance, a benefit to human civilization and the biosphere it depends on.

---

Chinese imperial records describe citrus cultivation hundreds of kilometers north of its modern range. No coal plants, no SUVs, no industrial emissions — and yet a warming of regional, and very likely global, significance. Before that came the Roman Warm Period. Before that, the Holocene Climatic Optimum, warmer still. Between these warm intervals came cold ones: the Little Ice Age that froze the Thames, the Dark Ages cold period that coincided with mass migration and plague. The pattern is millennial, rhythmic, and indifferent to human activity.

What drives these cycles? The honest answer is that we do not fully know — but we have strong candidates, and none of them are anthropogenic. The sun is the engine of Earth's climate, and it is not a constant star. Solar magnetic activity modulates cosmic ray flux, which in turn appears to influence low cloud formation — a mechanism explored by Svensmark and others that the official bodies have been too quick to dismiss. Grand solar minima like the Maunder and Dalton correlate strikingly with cold periods. To assume solar variability is negligible because a particular computer model says so is not science; it is circular reasoning.

The Pacific Decadal Oscillation, the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation, the ENSO cycle — these are vast reservoirs of heat whose phase shifts rearrange global temperature patterns on timescales of decades to centuries. The "regional anomaly" defense is a retreat maintained only by weighting proxies selectively and dismissing inconvenient ones. Before the Medieval Warm Period came the Roman Warm Period; before that, the Holocene Climatic Optimum, warmer still. Between them came the Little Ice Age, the Dark Ages cold period. The pattern is millennial, rhythmic, and indifferent to human activity.

What drives these cycles? The sun is the engine of Earth's climate, and it is not a constant star. Critics note that satellite measurements of total solar irradiance show only modest variation since the 1970s and argue this rules out a solar role in modern warming. But total irradiance is not the whole story. Solar magnetic activity modulates cosmic ray flux, which influences low cloud formation — a mechanism pioneered by Svensmark. Spectral changes in ultraviolet output, far larger than changes in total irradiance, alter stratospheric chemistry and circulation in ways climate models capture poorly. Grand solar minima like the Maunder and Dalton correlate strikingly with cold periods. To treat the sun as a near-constant because one narrow metric appears steady is to mistake a single instrument reading for the whole orchestra.

Then there are the oceans. The Pacific Decadal Oscillation, the Atlantic Multidecadal Oscillation, the ENSO cycle — vast reservoirs of heat whose phase shifts rearrange global temperature patterns on timescales of decades to centuries. Much of the late-twentieth-century warming routinely attributed to CO₂ coincided with a positive phase of the AMO and a string of powerful El Niños.

The satellites, which cover the whole planet rather than the uneven land-based grid, consistently show warming trends substantially lower than those reported by surface datasets like GISS or HadCRUT. This discrepancy is not a rounding error. It is a systematic divergence, and it has grown as surface datasets have undergone repeated "adjustments" — almost always in the direction of cooling the past and warming the present.

The satellites and weather balloons have looked for this hot spot for thirty years. It is not there, or it is present so weakly that the models are plainly overstating the water-vapor feedback that drives their high sensitivity estimates. The satellite record is consistent with a climate sensitivity near 1.5°C per doubling of CO₂, not the 3°C or higher favored by the most alarming models. A lower-sensitivity climate is a climate we can live with and adapt to, not one demanding the wholesale reorganization of civilization.

And alarm has become the currency of this debate. We are told hurricanes are worsening — the IPCC itself finds no clear trend in global tropical cyclone frequency. We are told wildfires prove the crisis — yet global burned area has declined over recent decades, a fact rarely reported. We are told small island nations are vanishing — tide gauges show sea level rising at roughly 3 millimeters per year, a rate consistent with the post-Little Ice Age recovery that began long before industrial emissions mattered, with no clear acceleration attributable to anthropogenic forcing once natural variability is accounted for.

The ice advanced and retreated through a hundred cycles before our species learned to smelt metal. To attribute the current, modest warming primarily to our tailpipes is to mistake a ripple for the tide.

The policy consequences of getting this wrong are enormous. Trillions of dollars are being directed toward the mitigation of a problem whose magnitude is uncertain and whose natural component is being systematically understated. Those trillions could instead address malaria, clean water, agricultural resilience, and adaptation to the climate variability that will occur regardless of what we do to our emissions.

The climate is changing. It always has. The most unscientific position of all is to pretend that this time, uniquely in the planet's four-billion-year history, the cause must be us.
